passages.explanation: Jesus tells a parable to show that they should always pray and not give up. In a certain town there's a judge who neither fears God nor cares what people think. A widow in that town keeps coming to him: grant me justice against my adversary. For some time he refuses. But finally he thinks: even though I don't fear God or care about people, this widow keeps bothering me — I'll see that she gets justice, so she won't wear me out. Jesus says: listen to what the unjust judge says. Won't God bring about justice for his chosen ones who cry out to him day and night? Will he keep putting them off? He'll see that they get justice, and quickly. However, when the Son of Man comes, will he find faith on the earth? The logic mirrors the midnight friend parable in chapter 11: if an unjust judge responds to persistence, how much more will a just God? The final question is haunting — the issue isn't whether God will act, but whether anyone will still be trusting him when he does.

To some who are confident of their own righteousness and look down on everyone else, Jesus tells this parable: two men go up to the temple to pray. One is a Pharisee, the other a tax collector. The Pharisee stands by himself and prays: God, I thank you that I'm not like other people — robbers, evildoers, adulterers — or even like this tax collector. I fast twice a week and give a tenth of all I get. But the tax collector stands at a distance. He won't even look up to heaven but beats his breast: God, have mercy on me, a sinner. Jesus says: this man, rather than the other, went home justified before God. For all who exalt themselves will be humbled, and those who humble themselves will be exalted. The Pharisee's prayer is a performance review addressed to himself. The tax collector's prayer is the only kind that reaches God — honest desperation.

People are bringing babies to Jesus for him to place his hands on them. The disciples rebuke them. When Jesus sees this, he's indignant: let the little children come to me, and don't hinder them, for the kingdom of God belongs to such as these. Truly I tell you, anyone who won't receive the kingdom of God like a little child will never enter it. Children can't earn, achieve, or negotiate their way into anything — they can only receive. That posture is the entry point for the kingdom.

A certain ruler asks: good teacher, what must I do to inherit eternal life? Jesus asks: why do you call me good? No one is good except God alone. You know the commandments: don't commit adultery, don't murder, don't steal, don't give false testimony, honour your father and mother. The ruler says: all these I've kept since I was a boy. When Jesus hears this, he says: you still lack one thing. Sell everything you have and give to the poor, and you'll have treasure in heaven. Then come, follow me. The man becomes very sad because he's extremely wealthy. Jesus looks at him and says: how hard it is for the rich to enter the kingdom of God! It's easier for a camel to go through the eye of a needle than for someone who is rich to enter the kingdom of God. Those who hear this ask: who then can be saved? Jesus replies: what is impossible with man is possible with God. Peter says: we've left everything to follow you. Jesus says: truly I tell you, no one who has left home or wife or brothers or sisters or parents or children for the sake of the kingdom of God will fail to receive many times as much in this age, and in the age to come eternal life. Jesus doesn't demand poverty from everyone — he identifies this man's specific idol. Whatever you can't give up is the thing that owns you.

Jesus takes the Twelve aside and tells them: we're going up to Jerusalem, and everything written by the prophets about the Son of Man will be fulfilled. He'll be handed over to the Gentiles. He'll be mocked, insulted, spat on, and flogged, and they'll kill him. On the third day he'll rise again. The disciples don't understand any of this. Its meaning is hidden from them, and they don't know what he's talking about. This is the third passion prediction. Jesus is explicit — betrayal, Gentile involvement, torture, death, resurrection. The disciples' incomprehension isn't intellectual; it's conceptual. They can't fit a suffering Messiah into their framework.

As Jesus approaches Jericho, a blind man is sitting by the road begging. He hears the crowd going by and asks what's happening. They tell him Jesus of Nazareth is passing by. He shouts: Jesus, Son of David, have mercy on me! Those in front tell him to be quiet, but he shouts louder: Son of David, have mercy on me! Jesus stops and orders the man brought to him. When he comes near, Jesus asks: what do you want me to do for you? Lord, I want to see, he says. Jesus says: receive your sight — your faith has healed you. Immediately he receives his sight and follows Jesus, praising God. Everyone who sees it praises God. The blind man sees what the disciples can't — he calls Jesus 'Son of David,' a messianic title. The one who is physically blind has spiritual sight, while those with eyes can't understand the passion predictions.
